content.problemStatement:
You are given two arrays AAA and BBB, of lengths NNN and MMM respectively (Mв‰¤NM \leq NMв‰¤N).

All the elements of AAA are distinct, and all the elements of BBB are distinct вЂ” that is, Aiв‰ AjA_i \neq A_jAiвЂ‹оЂ =AjвЂ‹ for iв‰ ji \neq jiоЂ =j, and Biв‰ BjB_i \neq B_jBiвЂ‹оЂ =BjвЂ‹ for iв‰ ji \neq jiоЂ =j.

In one operation, you can do the following:


Choose a subarrayвЂ ^\daggerвЂ  of AAA with length MMM, and replace it with a cyclic rotationвЂЎ^\ddaggerвЂЎ of the array BBB.
For instance, if A=[3,2,5,4,6]A = [3, 2, 5, 4, 6]A=[3,2,5,4,6] and B=[2,1,3]B = [2, 1, 3]B=[2,1,3], you can choose to replace the subarray [2,5,4][2, 5, 4][2,5,4] of AAA with the array [3,2,1][3, 2, 1][3,2,1] which is a cyclic shift of BBB, to obtain [3,3,2,1,6][3, 3, 2, 1, 6][3,3,2,1,6].

Your task is to find the **lexicographically minimum** possible array AAA that can be obtained by performing this operation several (possibly, zero) times.

An array PPP of length NNN is lexicographically smaller than another array QQQ of the same length if and only if there exists an index iii (1в‰¤iв‰¤N1 \leq i \leq N1в‰¤iв‰¤N) such that:


Pi<QiP_i \lt Q_iPiвЂ‹<QiвЂ‹; and
Pj=QjP_j = Q_jPjвЂ‹=QjвЂ‹ for all 1в‰¤j<i1 \leq j \lt i1в‰¤j<i
вЂ ^\daggerвЂ  A subarray of an array is obtained by deleting some (possibly zero) elements from its prefix and some (possibly zero) elements from its suffix.

For example, if A=[1,3,4,2]A = [1, 3, 4, 2]A=[1,3,4,2] then [1],[3,4,2],[1], [3, 4, 2],[1],[3,4,2], and [1,3][1, 3][1,3] are some of its subarrays; whereas [1,4][1, 4][1,4] is not a subarray of AAA.

вЂЎ^\ddaggerвЂЎ A cyclic rotation of an array is obtained by repeatedly moving its first element to its end, zero or more times.

For example, the cyclic rotations of [1,3,4,2][1, 3, 4, 2][1,3,4,2] are [1,3,4,2],[3,4,2,1],[4,2,1,3],[2,1,3,4][1, 3, 4, 2], [3, 4, 2, 1], [4, 2, 1, 3], [2, 1, 3, 4][1,3,4,2],[3,4,2,1],[4,2,1,3],[2,1,3,4].


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM вЂ” the lengths of the arrays AAA and BBB, respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,вЂ¦,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1вЂ‹,A2вЂ‹,вЂ¦,ANвЂ‹ вЂ” the elements of
array AAA.
The third line contains MMM space-separated integers B1,B2,вЂ¦,BMB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_MB1вЂ‹,B2вЂ‹,вЂ¦,BMвЂ‹ вЂ” the elements of array BBB.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line NNN space-separated integers, denoting the lexicographically minimum array AAA that can be obtained by performing the given operation several times.
constraints: Aiв‰ AjA_i \neq A_jAiвЂ‹оЂ =AjвЂ‹ for iв‰ ji \neq jiоЂ =j
Biв‰ BjB_i \neq B_jBiвЂ‹оЂ =BjвЂ‹ for iв‰ ji \neq jiоЂ =j
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2в‹…1052\cdot 10^52в‹…105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Perform these two operations:

Replace subarray [4][4][4] of AAA with [2][2][2], to obtain [2,1,6][2, 1, 6][2,1,6].
Replace subarray [6][6][6] of AAA with [2][2][2], to obtain [2,1,2][2, 1, 2][2,1,2].

This is the lexicographically smallest array that can be obtained.
Test case 222: It's optimal to not perform any moves.
Test case 333: Perform one operation:

Replace subarray [3,7,1][3, 7, 1][3,7,1] of AAA with [3,6,4][3, 6, 4][3,6,4] (which is a cyclic rotation of BBB) to obtain [2,3,6,4][2, 3, 6, 4][2,3,6,4].

This is optimal.
